name: Interactive Brokers
description_short: Professional-grade brokerage with the lowest commissions, global market access, and powerful Trader Workstation platform.
description_long:
## What Interactive Brokers Is

Interactive Brokers (IBKR) is a US-listed brokerage firm founded in 1978 by Thomas Peterffy, a Hungarian-born programmer who essentially invented electronic market making. The company is headquartered in Greenwich, Connecticut, trades on Nasdaq under ticker IBKR, and holds client equity of over $500 billion across more than 3 million client accounts globally. It is regulated in every major jurisdiction it operates in, including the SEC, FINRA, FCA, and MAS.

This is not a broker built for casual investors. Interactive Brokers is a professional-grade brokerage designed for active traders, quantitative funds, RIAs, and anyone who wants direct access to 150+ markets across 33 countries in 27 currencies. If you are looking for a friendly onboarding experience with cartoon confetti when you buy your first share, this is the wrong tool.

## Core Features and What Makes It Unique

The centerpiece is **Trader Workstation (TWS)**, a Java-based desktop platform that has been the standard for professional retail traders for two decades. It is dense, ugly, and infinitely configurable. You get access to stocks, ETFs, options, futures, forex, bonds, mutual funds, crypto, spot gold, prediction markets (recently launched), and even hedge funds through their Investors' Marketplace.

Beyond TWS, Interactive Brokers offers **IBKR Mobile**, **Client Portal** (web), and **IBKR GlobalTrader** for a simpler mobile-first experience. The **APIs** are a major differentiator — REST, FIX, TWS API, and their newer Web API all give algorithmic traders real production-grade access. Very few retail-accessible brokers offer this depth.

Other standouts include **IBKR ATS** (their alternative trading system for price improvement), **SmartRouting** for order execution, **Probability Lab** for options analysis, **PortfolioAnalyst** for performance tracking, and a **Stock Yield Enhancement Program** that pays you to lend out shares. Margin rates are consistently the lowest in the retail brokerage industry — often 1.5-2% below what Schwab or Fidelity charge.

## Pricing Breakdown

Interactive Brokers has two pricing structures:

**IBKR Lite** — Commission-free trading on US-listed stocks and ETFs. Options are $0.65 per contract. No account minimum. This is the direct answer to Robinhood and is only available to US residents. You give up SmartRouting and get payment-for-order-flow execution instead.

**IBKR Pro** — The real product. Stocks and ETFs are $0.005 per share (fixed pricing) with a $1 minimum and 1% of trade value maximum. Volume tiered pricing drops as low as $0.0005/share for traders doing 100M+ shares/month. Options range from **$0.15 to $0.65 per contract** depending on premium and volume. Futures are $0.25 to $0.85 per contract. Forex is 0.08 to 0.20 basis points. No account minimum, no inactivity fees (removed in 2021).

Margin rates on IBKR Pro currently sit around benchmark + 1.5% for balances under $100K, dropping to benchmark + 0.5% for balances over $1M. Check their website for current rates as these move with the Fed.

Market data is where costs sneak up on people. US equities data is roughly $4.50-$14/month depending on the bundle, and international exchange feeds add up fast if you trade globally.

## Honest Pros

**Lowest all-in costs for active traders.** Between commissions, margin rates, and execution quality via SmartRouting/IBKR ATS, nobody beats Interactive Brokers on total cost of ownership for traders doing meaningful volume.

**Genuine global market access.** 150 markets, 33 countries, 27 currencies. You can buy Japanese stocks, German bunds, Hong Kong futures, and US options from a single account. No other retail broker comes close.

**Serious API infrastructure.** If you want to run automated strategies without paying for a hedge fund prime broker, IBKR is the answer. The API is battle-tested and used by thousands of quant firms.

**Financial strength.** IBKR has over $16 billion in equity capital, is publicly traded, and has never had a serious solvency scare. In an industry where broker failures happen, this matters.

**Rare product breadth.** Prediction markets, forecast contracts, bonds marketplace, mutual funds, hedge fund access — all under one login.

**Interest on idle cash.** IBKR Pro pays USD Fed Funds minus 0.5% on balances over $10K, which is dramatically better than most competitors.

## Honest Cons

**Trader Workstation is genuinely intimidating.** New users routinely spend weeks learning where things are. The UI feels like it was designed by engineers for engineers in 2004 and updated reluctantly since.

**Customer support is slow and inconsistent.** Chat and phone waits can stretch past an hour during volatile market conditions. The support team is technically competent but not friendly.

**Client Portal (web) is functionally weaker than TWS.** If you refuse to install the desktop app, you lose access to major features.

**Market data fees add up.** Unlike Schwab or Fidelity, you pay separately for most real-time data subscriptions, and international feeds can cost $50-100/month combined.

**Not beginner-friendly.** Account approval workflows, margin/portfolio-margin distinctions, tax treatment options, and the sheer number of order types will overwhelm someone new to trading.

**IBKR Lite is a compromised product.** No SmartRouting means execution quality drops materially. If you are considering Lite, you probably belong at a different broker.

## Who Should Use Interactive Brokers

Use Interactive Brokers if you are an **active trader doing 20+ trades per month**, an **options or futures trader** who cares about per-contract cost and fill quality, an **international trader** needing access outside the US, a **quant or systematic trader** building automated systems, or a **high-net-worth investor** who wants margin rates that don't cost 12%.

Skip Interactive Brokers if you are a **buy-and-hold ETF investor** (Fidelity or Schwab will treat you better for free), a **complete beginner** (the platform will crush your motivation), or someone who values **hand-holding customer service** (they will disappoint you).

## How It Compares to Charles Schwab and tastytrade

**Interactive Brokers vs Charles Schwab.** Schwab is the industry standard for US-focused retail investors and offers better research tools, better customer service, and thinkorswim (which is a superior charting platform to TWS). But Schwab charges $0.65/contract on options with no volume discounts, has significantly higher margin rates, and offers a fraction of the international access. If you trade actively or globally, Interactive Brokers wins on cost. If you are a US-only investor who values service, Schwab wins.

**Interactive Brokers vs tastytrade.** tastytrade is built specifically for options traders and offers a cleaner, more purpose-built platform with excellent educational content. Their pricing is competitive ($1.00 to open options, $0 to close, capped at $10/leg). For pure US options traders who want a modern UI and don't need global access, tastytrade is often the better fit. Interactive Brokers wins on multi-asset trading, international markets, margin rates, and API access.

## The Bottom Line

Interactive Brokers is the correct answer for serious traders and the wrong answer for casual ones. The pricing is genuinely the lowest in the industry once you get past IBKR Lite marketing gimmicks. The platform is powerful but user-hostile. The company is financially rock-solid and has been building trading infrastructure longer than most of its competitors have existed.

If your trading is a hobby, use Fidelity or Schwab. If your trading is a profession or a serious side pursuit, Interactive Brokers is difficult to beat and probably worth the learning curve. Rating stands at **4.6/5** — the deductions are entirely about usability and support, not capability.
pros: Lowest commissions and margin rates in the industry for active traders
Access to 150+ markets in 33 countries and 27 currencies
Production-grade REST, FIX, and TWS APIs for automated trading
Financially strong, publicly traded with $16B+ in equity capital
Pays competitive interest on idle USD cash over $10K
Rare breadth: stocks, options, futures, bonds, forex, crypto, prediction markets
cons: Trader Workstation has a genuinely steep learning curve
Customer support is slow with long wait times during volatile markets
Real-time market data fees add up quickly for global traders
Client Portal web app is functionally weaker than the desktop TWS
IBKR Lite loses SmartRouting and delivers worse execution quality
best_for: Active traders doing 20+ trades per month
Options and futures traders focused on cost per contract
International traders needing global market access
Quant and systematic traders building on APIs
High-net-worth investors seeking low margin rates
